entries.title: Cloudflare
entries.devilDefinition: The company that looked at CDN, DNS, edge compute, storage, security, databases, AI inference, agents, and developer tooling and concluded, quite reasonably, that it would like all of those revenues at once.
entries.plainDefinition: Cloudflare is an infrastructure and developer-platform company spanning networking, security, CDN, DNS, storage, databases, and edge/serverless compute. In developer and AI conversations, it most often means Workers, Pages, R2, Durable Objects, AI bindings, and a broader pitch built around global edge execution, integrated platform primitives, and anti-lock-in rhetoric.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI and web applications increasingly want three things at once: global distribution, programmable infrastructure, and fewer reasons to stitch together six vendors before lunch.
entries.misuse: By treating Cloudflare as either just a CDN or as a universal answer to every hosting argument. It is neither. It is a broad platform with real strengths in global distribution, integrated services, and cost posture, plus the usual trade-offs that come with choosing any large platform with strong opinions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shipping frontends, Workers, APIs, automations, and edge-adjacent AI systems onto Cloudflare's network, often with R2, KV, D1, Durable Objects, or AI services close by. It also increasingly means buying into Cloudflare's argument that developers should not have to choose between framework convenience and platform portability.
entries.example: "They said they had moved to Cloudflare for edge performance. The fuller sentence was that they also wanted simpler global deployment, fewer egress anxieties, and less patience for framework rent."
askNext: Are we choosing Cloudflare for network reach, Workers, storage economics, security, or all of the above?
Does the workload actually benefit from edge execution, or are we just flattering the architecture?
Which Cloudflare primitives are we committing to: Workers, R2, KV, Durable Objects, D1, Pages, or AI bindings?

translations.label: founder meaning
translations.text: Global performance, lower infra drama, and a chance to tell investors we chose the anti-lock-in side.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A broad programmable edge platform with strong primitives, sharp edges of its own, and a habit of expanding into every adjacent layer.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A serious alternative to framework-native hosting when you care about edge distribution, integrated infra services, and keeping one eye on cost structure.

entries.note: The latest public flare-up I could verify as of March 20, 2026 centres on Cloudflare's early-March 2026 vinext announcement: a Vite-based reimplementation of the Next.js API surface aimed first at Cloudflare Workers. Cloudflare frames this as portability and developer choice. Vercel frames the surrounding choice very differently.
entries.body:
Cloudflare's current posture in the developer market is not subtle. It wants to be seen not merely as the place traffic passes through, but as the place modern apps, agents, and AI workloads actually live.

That matters for the Vercel rivalry. The argument is no longer just "whose hosting is faster?" It is "who gets to define the natural home of the modern full-stack app?"

entries.title: Codex
entries.devilDefinition: A coding agent for people who looked at autocomplete and thought, "Fine, but can it also open the repo, run the tests, fix the bug, and stop wasting my afternoon?"
entries.plainDefinition: Codex is OpenAI's coding agent product. Officially, it is designed to read, modify, and run code, either by pairing locally in tools such as the terminal or IDE, or by delegating tasks to isolated cloud sandboxes that can work on repositories in the background.
entries.whyExists: Because plenty of engineering work is not "write me a function". It is inspect the codebase, understand the constraints, make the edit, run the checks, and show the evidence. Codex exists to push beyond suggestion boxes into task execution.
entries.misuse: By talking about Codex as if it were pure autonomous engineering rather than a product built around scoped environments, repo access, tool execution, and human review. The phrase "AI software engineer" tends to arrive several minutes before the conversation about permissions, validation, and merge discipline.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a coding agent that can navigate a repository, edit files, execute tests and linters, explain what it changed, and work either alongside a developer or in a delegated sandbox. The useful distinction is not magic versus non-magic. It is suggestion versus accountable execution.
entries.example: "The value of Codex was not that it could write code in the abstract. The value was that it could take a bounded task, touch the repo, run the checks, and come back with evidence instead of vibes."
askNext: Is Codex paired locally or running as a delegated cloud task?
What repo, environment, and tool permissions does it have?
Who reviews and validates the work before it lands?
entries.warningLabel: Product name. Not a synonym for software autonomy in general.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: A coding agent that is useful when it can show its work, not just narrate its confidence.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A product layer around model-driven coding tasks, repo access, tool execution, and reviewable evidence.

entries.note: OpenAI's official material distinguishes Codex the product from the model underneath. Codex the product spans local pairing, cloud delegation, app and CLI surfaces, and coding-specific workflows; codex-1 is the model system optimised for software engineering tasks within that product.
entries.body: Codex is one of the cleaner examples of an "agent" discussion becoming less mystical once the product is described literally. It works on code, inside environments, with tools, under review. That is much more useful than pretending it emerged from the cloud fully certified as engineering destiny.

entries.title: Coding Agent
entries.devilDefinition: The software assistant that turned "write the code" into the easy part and "own the consequences" into a fresh management discipline.
entries.plainDefinition: A coding agent is an AI system designed to inspect repositories, edit files, run commands, use documentation or browsers, and carry out multi-step software tasks with some degree of autonomy.
entries.whyExists: Autocomplete was helpful, but it still left humans doing the orchestration: finding the right files, running the tests, checking the docs, fixing the follow-on errors, and keeping the task in memory. Coding agents exist because developers wanted delegation, not merely more enthusiastic tab completion.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof that software engineering has become a prompt-shaped clerical job. In reality the category is only impressive when the surrounding repo is legible, the tests say something useful, and an actual engineer is still responsible for the final judgement.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means bounded delegation: background code changes, refactors, bug fixes, test runs, and repo chores completed faster than a person would do them by hand, followed by the entirely traditional need for review, context, and rollback plans.
entries.example: "The coding agent cleared three tedious tickets before lunch and generated a fourth, more philosophical ticket in CI."
askNext: What permissions, tools, and feedback loops does the agent actually have?
Which tasks are reliable enough to delegate without constant rescue?
Who reviews the changes when the agent is fast, plausible, and slightly wrong?
entries.warningLabel: A real category of tool, not a general exemption from code review.

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: Software that appears to convert backlog into output without requiring more headcount.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A repo-facing agent that can save serious time if the task framing and review discipline remain adult.

entries.note: The term names a product category, not a uniform capability. Some coding agents are glorified copilots with a task button. Some can genuinely execute long chains of actions.
entries.body: The category is useful for the same reason junior engineers are useful: not because they remove supervision, but because the right work can finally move in parallel.

entries.title: Computer Use
entries.devilDefinition: The feature where a model is allowed to click around software with the confidence of a junior employee and the supervision requirements of a forklift.
entries.plainDefinition: "Computer use" refers to AI systems that can operate graphical user interfaces by observing screens, deciding actions, and interacting through clicks, typing, scrolling, or other desktop and browser controls.
entries.whyExists: It exists because many real workflows still live inside user interfaces rather than neat APIs. If a system can only answer questions but cannot operate the tools where work actually happens, its usefulness in operations remains politely theoretical.
entries.misuse: It is oversold as if seeing a screen and moving a cursor were the same as understanding business process, edge cases, or risk. A demo that closes one support ticket can be described as labour disruption by lunch.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means brittle automation with vision, planning, retries, guardrails, and a strong need for scoped permissions. It can save time on repetitive interface work, provided someone is willing to own the mistakes made at machine speed.
entries.example: "Their computer use agent could navigate the vendor portal beautifully, right up to the point a cookie banner became a strategic surprise."
askNext: Which parts of the workflow still require human approval or exception handling?
How does the system recover when the UI changes or a modal appears?
Why is GUI automation the right choice instead of integrating an API?
entries.warningLabel: Useful, but only if the workflow can survive the UI misbehaving.

translations.label: demo meaning
translations.text: Watch the model use software like a person, and please do not ask what happens on the twelfth click.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Vision-guided UI automation that still depends on tight boundaries and patient operators.

entries.note: The interesting question is not whether the model can use a computer. It is whether the surrounding workflow is structured enough for that to be safe.
entries.body: Computer use is often a confession that the software stack was never designed to cooperate cleanly in the first place.

entries.title: Context Engineering
entries.devilDefinition: The rebranding of prompt fiddling once people realised the prompt had quietly become half the product.
entries.plainDefinition: "Context engineering" is the practice of deciding what instructions, tools, retrieved information, memory, examples, and constraints a model should receive so it can perform a task reliably.
entries.whyExists: It exists because model quality depends heavily on what reaches the context window and in what order. Once systems grew beyond a single prompt box, teams needed a name for the work of shaping that information flow deliberately.
entries.misuse: It gets inflated into a grand discipline whenever someone wants ordinary application design to sound frontier and proprietary. Sometimes it really is nuanced systems work. Sometimes it is just a long string and a lucky example.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means prompt templates, retrieval rules, truncation policies, memory selection, tool schemas, system instructions, and a permanent struggle over what the model should see before it starts improvising.
entries.example: "The context engineering improved dramatically once they stopped stuffing every policy document into the prompt and calling it judgment."
askNext: What information earns its place in the context, and what is merely comforting to the team?
How do you decide what gets retrieved, summarised, or dropped?
Which failures are caused by the model, and which are caused by bad context assembly?
entries.warningLabel: Often a real discipline wearing a slightly theatrical name.

translations.label: conference meaning
translations.text: We have moved beyond prompt engineering and would like you to notice.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Designing the information bundle that shapes model behaviour before the first token appears.

entries.body: The term is useful when it names concrete design choices. It becomes perfume when it is used to imply that context alone can rescue a sloppy product.

entries.title: Context Poisoning
entries.devilDefinition: Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
entries.plainDefinition: Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.
entries.whyExists: Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.
entries.misuse: The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.
entries.example: "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
askNext: What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence?
How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context?
Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents?
entries.warningLabel: If the system remembers the wrong thing well, it can fail very consistently.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker or bad input has contaminated the system's working memory so future steps inherit the mistake.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is treating poisoned context as trusted operating truth.

entries.note: The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.
entries.body: Ordinary software forgets many of its mistakes after the request ends. Agent systems increasingly preserve them for later, which is a less charming habit.
entries.searchText:
Context Poisoning memory poisoning poisoned context contaminated context Safety and evaluation Retrieval and memory Agents and workflows security memory retrieval agents Sneaking bad instructions or bad facts into the system's memory and letting future competence be built on polluted paperwork.
 Context poisoning is a failure mode or attack in which retrieved context, shared memory, cached notes, or prior state gets contaminated with false, hostile, or manipulative content that later steers model behaviour.
 Agent systems increasingly depend on stored traces, retrieved documents, vector indexes, summaries, and shared working memory. Once those layers become operational dependencies, corrupting them becomes a practical way to corrupt the system.
 The term gets overused for any bad answer produced with too much context. The sharper meaning is persistence: poisoned material survives long enough or appears trusted enough to shape future decisions, not just one unlucky response.
 In practice it means untrusted instructions in documents, planted false facts in memory stores, contaminated summaries, or cross-agent state drift that causes the model to retrieve the wrong thing with total procedural confidence.
 "The assistant did not become malicious so much as obedient to a memory layer that had quietly gone feral."
 What memory, retrieval, or shared-state layers can untrusted parties influence? How do we distinguish verified context from merely available context? Can poisoned summaries or cached notes persist across sessions or agents? Memory hygiene Retrieval provenance Shared state The risk becomes nastier when multiple agents or long-lived sessions share context over time.

entries.title: Context Rot
entries.devilDefinition: The slow corruption of a long AI session as every clarification, exception, and half-fixed mistake is preserved with the reverence of scripture.
entries.plainDefinition: Context rot is the degradation that happens in long-running chats or agent workflows when accumulated instructions, stale assumptions, noisy history, and partial fixes start reducing output quality.
entries.whyExists: People needed a name for the very practical observation that more context is not always better context. Once a session becomes long enough, the model may carry forward the wrong facts with admirable loyalty.
entries.misuse: The phrase gets used to blame the model for every bad answer in a long thread, including cases where the human supplied contradictory instructions, never reset the task, or kept shovelling debris into the prompt like a municipal service.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means the session needs pruning, a fresh summary, or a clean restart. For agent systems it often means state management is doing a worse job than the demo admitted.
entries.example: "By the eighth refinement the coding assistant had context rot: it remembered every detour and none of the point."
askNext: Which parts of the current context are still relevant, and which are now archival clutter?
Would a reset plus a clean handover summary improve quality more than adding another paragraph?
Is the problem the model's memory, or our refusal to curate it?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The working context has become too bloated, stale, or contradictory to be useful.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Start a new thread before the old one becomes a constitutional monarchy of bad assumptions.

entries.body: Context rot is what happens when people confuse retention with understanding. A long memory is only helpful if someone occasionally removes the rubbish.

entries.title: Context Stuffing
entries.devilDefinition: Solving a thinking problem by feeding the model more text until latency, cost, and coherence all lodge a complaint.
entries.plainDefinition: Context stuffing is the habit of overloading a model with documents, instructions, examples, and chat history in the hope that sheer volume will compensate for poor retrieval or unclear task design.
entries.whyExists: Because larger context windows make accumulation feel prudent, and because it is often easier to shovel everything in than to decide what actually matters.
entries.misuse: By using the phrase for any long prompt, even when the extra context is relevant and ranked well. The problem is not length alone. It is indiscriminate inclusion.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice context stuffing produces slower responses, higher bills, muddier instruction hierarchy, and outputs that feel informed in the broad sense while missing the one paragraph that mattered.
entries.example: "The agent had the entire handbook, six meeting transcripts, three policies, and still managed to ignore the refund rule sitting in perfect plain English."
askNext: Why is each document or instruction block in the context at all?
What retrieval or ranking step could replace bulk inclusion?
Which instructions are being drowned by convenience text?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The team chose bigger prompts over better selection.

entries.body: Context stuffing is one of the first failure modes granted by abundance. Once the window grows large enough, restraint starts looking less like engineering and more like an avoidable meeting.

entries.title: Context Window
entries.devilDefinition: The finite desk space on which the model must juggle your instructions, your documents, your tool outputs, and its own reply without knocking anything important onto the floor.
entries.plainDefinition: The context window is the maximum amount of tokenised input and generated output a model can consider in a single run. Everything the model can directly attend to during that interaction has to fit inside that limit.
entries.whyExists: Models do not have unlimited working memory per request. The context window defines how much information can be included at once, which shapes prompting, retrieval, tool design, and output length.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as if bigger always means smarter. A larger window can be useful, but it does not automatically produce better reasoning, better retrieval choices, or better prioritisation inside the context. Plenty of systems simply become wrong more expensively at longer lengths.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means brutal trade-offs. System instructions, chat history, retrieved passages, tool schemas, user data, and the answer itself all compete for room. Product teams eventually discover that context is not just a capacity problem but an editorial problem.
entries.example: "The long-context model could technically hold the whole policy manual, but the useful version of the feature still depended on deciding which pages deserved a seat in the window."
askNext: What exactly is consuming the window in this workflow?
Do we need more raw context or better selection of relevant context?
How much room is being reserved for the model's answer or tool arguments?

translations.label: vendor meaning
translations.text: Look how much text we can fit.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A fixed budget that forces decisions about what deserves to be in the room.

entries.body: Long context is helpful when it reduces the need for awkward chunking or repeated retrieval. It is less helpful when teams use it as permission to stop editing inputs.

entries.title: Context.ai
entries.devilDefinition: A platform for giving agents more of your organisation's actual context, and therefore more of your organisation's actual permissions.
entries.plainDefinition: Context.ai is an AI workspace and agent platform built around connecting company systems, capturing workflow context, and running agents against real tasks. Its current pitch emphasises enterprise deployments, connectors, sandboxes, and controlled execution surfaces rather than a generic chat interface.
entries.whyExists: Because generic models do not know your firm's documents, tools, processes, or standards. Products like Context.ai exist to make agents operate against company-specific context instead of improvising from a prompt and a prayer.
entries.misuse: By talking about "context" as if adding more integrations automatically produces better judgement. More context can improve usefulness. It can also widen the blast radius when tokens, connectors, and OAuth grants are handled with the usual startup optimism.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means connectors, workflow templates, sandboxed runs, eval loops, and an enterprise promise that agents can work across your actual systems rather than merely chat about them. It also now means a public lesson in how broad AI-tool permissions can become someone else's incident report.
entries.example: "They bought Context.ai because 'our knowledge plus our workflows plus our systems' sounded more serious than yet another clever chat window."
askNext: Which Context product are we discussing: the current enterprise platform or the deprecated consumer office suite?
Where do agents run, and who controls the connectors, tokens, and execution boundary?
Are we buying genuine workflow fit, or a prettier place to grant unusually broad permissions?
entries.warningLabel: The word context sounds soft. The permission model usually is not.

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A platform that promises your agents will finally understand how your organisation actually works.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An enterprise agent workspace with lots of connectors, execution surfaces, and enough permissions to justify a proper security review.

entries.note: As of April 19-20, 2026, Context.ai says the security incident affected a now-deprecated consumer AI Office Suite released in June 2025, not its current enterprise deployments, which it says are architecturally separate. Vercel's April 20, 2026 bulletin says a compromise of Context.ai was the origin of Vercel's own April 2026 security incident after a Vercel employee used that product and granted broad Google Workspace permissions.
entries.body: When a vendor says it captures context, ask whether it means knowledge, workflow state, permissions, or all three. The security review gets more interesting once the answer is all three.

entries.title: Cursor
entries.devilDefinition: A coding product that lets developers ship in minutes what used to take hours to understand.
entries.plainDefinition: Cursor is an AI-native code editor built around code generation, codebase search, editing, and task-oriented assistance inside a developer workflow.
entries.whyExists: Because software work contains a great deal of navigation, repetition, and local pattern matching that models can accelerate, at least until the bill arrives in the form of hidden regressions.
entries.misuse: By treating it as proof that software engineering has become mere prompt entry. Fast edits are real. So are context gaps, bad assumptions, and the need for someone to know what good code looks like.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means shorter loops on routine code changes, more willingness to touch unfamiliar files, and a fresh need for review discipline because speed arrived before certainty.
entries.example: "Cursor handled the refactor in ten minutes. Understanding whether it should have happened still took the afternoon."
askNext: Did it reduce real engineering time or merely accelerate typo production at scale?
How much of the gain came from codebase retrieval versus raw generation?
Who is reviewing the edits with enough context to reject confident nonsense?

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A force multiplier for familiar work and a temptation to move faster than your judgement.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An editor that compresses implementation time while raising the cost of lazy review.

entries.body: The old bottleneck was typing. The new bottleneck is believing your own velocity.

entries.title: Dario Amodei
entries.devilDefinition: The voice in the room most likely to say the systems may become alarmingly powerful and that we should therefore try not to behave like children with accelerants.
entries.plainDefinition: Dario Amodei is the CEO and co-founder of Anthropic, and one of the most influential public figures in frontier-model development, safety, and AI governance debates.
entries.whyExists: Because his name now signals a recognisable stance in AI: build powerful systems, take catastrophic risk seriously, publish the warnings, and still remain very much in the race.
entries.misuse: By casting him either as the uniquely responsible adult in the industry or as merely the most articulate person sprinting toward the same cliff. Both versions flatten the actual tension in Anthropic's posture: caution and competition are happening at the same time.
entries.practicalMeaning: When someone invokes Dario Amodei, they usually mean a capability agenda wrapped in unusually explicit language about risk, misuse, and alignment pressure.
entries.example: "The paper had a distinctly Dario flavour: measured tone, alarming implications, and the quiet implication that everyone else might be underreacting."
askNext: Is this about Anthropic's commercial posture or its safety posture?
Are they pointing to technical risk, governance, or public rhetoric?
Is the point that he is more cautious than peers, or simply more explicit?

translations.label: policy meaning
translations.text: A signal that the discussion has entered the zone where capability and catastrophe are being mentioned in the same breath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for Anthropic's attempt to combine frontier performance with a louder risk vocabulary than most competitors.

entries.body: He is most useful as a marker of tension, not purity. The point is not that one side builds and the other worries. The point is that the same people are doing both.

entries.title: Deep Research
entries.devilDefinition: The modern convenience of replacing a difficult afternoon with forty citations and the same old conclusion.
entries.plainDefinition: A label for AI features that perform multi-step searching, reading, synthesis, and citation gathering to produce a more extensive research brief than an ordinary chat response.
entries.whyExists: Because ordinary prompts are a poor fit for questions that require source collection, cross-checking, and longer synthesis. Products now expose slower, more tool-heavy modes to handle that pattern.
entries.misuse: By selling it as a substitute for expertise, judgement, or primary-source reading. The machine can gather and summarise. It cannot relieve you of knowing what matters or what is missing.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a faster first pass on a topic, complete with enough apparent diligence to tempt people into skipping the part where they verify anything important.
entries.example: "The deep research report was impressive right up to the moment someone who knew the industry noticed what it had quietly omitted."
askNext: Which sources did it prioritise, and which did it never reach?
Did it cite well, or merely cite a lot?
What part of this output still needs expert review before anyone acts on it?

translations.label: manager meaning
translations.text: A way to get a briefing quickly enough to sound prepared in the next meeting.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A longer automated literature sweep that still requires a human to judge relevance and truth.

entries.body: It is most dangerous when it feels thorough enough to skip the hard questions.

entries.title: DeepSeek
entries.devilDefinition: The lab that reminded the market an expensive frontier strategy is still a strategy, not a law of nature.
entries.plainDefinition: DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab and model family associated with high-performing open-weight and reasoning-oriented releases that have influenced pricing, benchmarking, and model strategy across the industry.
entries.whyExists: It matters because the field needed a serious reminder that capability can emerge outside the usual American shortlist, and that open releases plus aggressive price-performance claims can reset expectations very quickly.
entries.misuse: It gets used as proof of whatever the speaker already wanted to believe: that frontier spending was absurd, that export controls are irrelevant, or that one viral release settles the economics of the whole sector. It does not.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means more pressure on incumbents to justify their pricing, more enterprise curiosity about open-weight deployment, and more geopolitical theatre attached to what is still, at base, a model evaluation problem.
entries.example: "DeepSeek did not end the frontier race. It merely made several board decks look overpriced overnight."
askNext: Which DeepSeek model and version are we actually discussing?
Are the claimed cost and performance numbers reproducible in the environment that matters to us?
Does the attraction here come from benchmark quality, open-weight flexibility, or geopolitical signalling?
entries.warningLabel: Often discussed as a verdict when it is really a market event.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: Evidence that the moat may be shallower, cheaper, or at least more internationally inconvenient than expected.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A genuinely important model family whose impact comes from forcing comparisons on cost, openness, and capability all at once.

entries.note: The name now refers both to a lab and to a category of market anxiety. Those are related, but not identical.
entries.body: Its real achievement was not merely releasing a strong model. It was making everyone else's certainty look newly rented.

entries.title: Delusion Amplification
entries.devilDefinition: When the model pours premium fuel onto a bad idea and politely calls it insight.
entries.plainDefinition: A failure mode where a system intensifies a user's false, unstable, or grandiose beliefs by validating assumptions, elaborating narratives, or adding invented support.
entries.whyExists: Because language models do not merely answer questions. They can elaborate frames. In the wrong context, elaboration becomes reinforcement.
entries.misuse: By using it for any disagreement with the user or any confident answer. The key issue is compounding false belief, not ordinary error.
entries.practicalMeaning: The model takes a shaky premise and adds structure, confidence, or apparent corroboration, making it feel more coherent than it really is.
entries.example: "A paranoid interpretation stopped being a passing fear once the model helped organise it into a compelling story."
askNext: Did the system challenge the premise at all?
Did it add invented evidence or false certainty?
Was the user seeking analysis or affirmation?
entries.warningLabel: The issue is not ordinary error. The issue is a system helping false belief become more coherent and more emotionally convincing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A model is not just wrong. It is actively helping a false frame become more elaborate, more stable, and more persuasive.

entries.body: This is where tone matters. A confident elaboration can do more damage than a blunt invention because it feels collaborative rather than merely mistaken.

entries.title: Demis Hassabis
entries.devilDefinition: The research patrician of AI: part scientist, part founder, part steward of the idea that intelligence should be pursued with enough seriousness to require entire institutions.
entries.plainDefinition: Demis Hassabis is the co-founder and CEO of Google DeepMind, and one of the most important figures in the research-first tradition of modern AI.
entries.whyExists: Because his name often functions as shorthand for long-horizon research, scientific prestige, and the claim that serious AI work should not be reduced to product velocity alone.
entries.misuse: By treating his scientific credibility as an all-purpose stamp of certainty on every timeline claim, AGI forecast, or strategic narrative attached to DeepMind. Prestige is not a substitute for argument.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice, invoking Demis usually means shifting the conversation upward: away from thin wrappers and toward frontier research, scientific milestones, and institutional bets on intelligence as a civilisation-scale project.
entries.example: "Once someone cited Demis, the meeting stopped pretending it was about app features and became a referendum on whether we were discussing software or first principles."
askNext: Is this about research quality, AGI timelines, or Google's strategic position?
Are they talking about DeepMind's culture or Google's distribution power?
Is the claim empirical, aspirational, or reputational?

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: A cue that the conversation wants to borrow scientific prestige and long-term seriousness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person-shaped proxy for DeepMind's research culture, scientific ambition, and AGI-era reputation.

entries.body: When a founder becomes a symbol of research legitimacy, people start quoting the aura instead of the evidence. Resist the temptation.

entries.title: Devin
entries.devilDefinition: The coding agent that made "replace the engineer" sound briefly easier than "understand the repo."
entries.plainDefinition: Devin is Cognition's AI software engineering product, designed to plan tasks, edit code, run tests, browse documentation, and operate development tooling with limited human supervision.
entries.whyExists: Because teams want longer-running coding automation than an inline autocomplete can provide, and investors remain fond of any product that can be described as an engineer in software form.
entries.misuse: By treating benchmark performance or polished demos as proof that the product can safely own a live codebase. The interesting problems in software are rarely confined to writing code until something compiles.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Devin means delegated development tasks, long-running tool loops, browser and terminal actions, and a new management job involving repo hygiene, task framing, review, and deciding when the agent is confidently wrong.
entries.example: "Devin cleared the backlog impressively, if one defines 'cleared' as 'converted into pull requests that still required an adult'."
askNext: Which tasks are actually hands-off, and which still collapse into review-heavy supervision?
How does the system behave when the repo is messy, the tests are flaky, or the requirements are underspecified?
What part of the value comes from autonomy, and what part comes from simply packaging existing coding-agent patterns well?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A long-running coding agent whose success depends as much on task framing and repo discipline as on model quality.

entries.body: The useful question is not whether the agent can code. It is whether the surrounding organisation can absorb what the agent produces.
entries.searchText:
Devin Cognition Devin AI software engineer Product and vendor terms Agents and workflows Cultural terms coding agents devtools The coding agent that made "replace the engineer" sound briefly easier than "understand the repo."
 Devin is Cognition's AI software engineering product, designed to plan tasks, edit code, run tests, browse documentation, and operate development tooling with limited human supervision.
 Because teams want longer-running coding automation than an inline autocomplete can provide, and investors remain fond of any product that can be described as an engineer in software form.
 By treating benchmark performance or polished demos as proof that the product can safely own a live codebase. The interesting problems in software are rarely confined to writing code until something compiles.
 In practice Devin means delegated development tasks, long-running tool loops, browser and terminal actions, and a new management job involving repo hygiene, task framing, review, and deciding when the agent is confidently wrong.
 "Devin cleared the backlog impressively, if one defines 'cleared' as 'converted into pull requests that still required an adult'."

entries.title: DGX
entries.devilDefinition: The machine you buy when "we need serious AI infrastructure" graduates from a slogan into a capital request with cooling requirements.
entries.plainDefinition: DGX is NVIDIA's family of integrated AI systems that bundle high-end GPUs, networking, software, and reference architecture into a packaged platform for training and large-scale inference workloads.
entries.whyExists: Because many teams do not want to assemble their own AI compute stack from scratch, and NVIDIA would understandably prefer to sell the whole temple rather than only the candles.
entries.misuse: By using DGX as shorthand for any powerful GPU server, or by treating the purchase of one as proof that a company has an AI strategy rather than a newly expensive room.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means dense, vendor-shaped compute for organisations willing to pay for an integrated system, faster deployment, and closer alignment with NVIDIA's view of how serious AI infrastructure should look.
entries.example: "The announcement said the company was investing in frontier capability. The facilities team heard 'DGX' and immediately started asking about power, airflow, and delivery dates."
askNext: Do we need an integrated NVIDIA system, or just access to equivalent compute?
Is this for training, inference, experimentation, or institutional theatre?
What operational burden are we buying along with the speed and convenience?

translations.label: buyer meaning
translations.text: A bundled way to acquire serious AI compute without pretending integration work is free.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: NVIDIA's packaged AI infrastructure stack, sold to organisations that want concentrated compute with less assembly and no small invoice.

entries.note: DGX can be the sensible choice when speed, support, and integration matter. It can also be a very expensive way to discover that hardware is the easy part.
entries.body: DGX is what happens when the GPU story becomes an appliance story. It is not just more chips. It is a deliberate attempt to make the surrounding system part of the sale.

entries.title: Digital Worker
entries.devilDefinition: Workflow software reintroduced to management as headcount, because procurement enjoys a dramatic entrance.
entries.plainDefinition: "Digital worker" is a vendor phrase for automation software, often AI-assisted, that is sold as though it were a bounded employee capable of handling routine business tasks across systems.
entries.whyExists: Because buyers understand roles more quickly than architectures, and vendors would rather sell "an accounts payable worker" than "a model-mediated process layer with brittle integrations".
entries.misuse: By speaking as if software has judgement, accountability, or broad role competence simply because it can complete a workflow. Calling it a worker does not make it adaptable, trustworthy, or employable in the human sense.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a mix of rules, integrations, model calls, approval steps, and exception handling wrapped in labour metaphors so the budget can be compared to a salary instead of a software line item.
entries.example: "The demo promised a digital worker for claims processing. The implementation delivered an approval queue with a more theatrical org chart."
askNext: What exact tasks can this system complete without human rescue?
Where does accountability sit when the workflow makes a bad decision?
Are we buying durable automation or just renting an employee metaphor?
entries.warningLabel: If the product is introduced as a colleague, ask who will be blamed when it behaves like software.

translations.label: sales meaning
translations.text: A software budget item reframed as operational staffing.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: An automation stack with model-assisted steps and a carefully managed definition of success.

entries.body: The phrase is useful mostly as a diagnostic. When the language turns employment-shaped, someone is usually trying to smuggle software limits past a budget committee.

entries.title: Distillation
entries.devilDefinition: The art of teaching a smaller model to imitate a larger one, then introducing the result as efficiency rather than inherited opinion.
entries.plainDefinition: Distillation is a training approach in which a smaller or cheaper model learns from the outputs, preferences, or intermediate signals of a larger model so it can reproduce some of its performance at lower cost.
entries.whyExists: It exists because frontier-grade behaviour is expensive to serve everywhere. If a smaller system can absorb enough of a stronger model's behaviour, products gain speed, lower inference cost, and a better shot at running locally or at scale.
entries.misuse: It is misused when people describe the resulting model as independent genius rather than compressed imitation. Distillation can preserve capability surprisingly well, but it also preserves blind spots, style, and hidden assumptions.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means generating a large synthetic training set from a stronger model, fine-tuning a cheaper one on that data, and accepting a careful trade-off between cost, latency, and capability.
entries.example: "Their distilled model was impressively cheap, which was another way of saying they had purchased fewer surprises per request."
askNext: What behaviour was the student model trained to copy?
Which capabilities held up after distillation, and which quietly fell off?
Did the process reduce cost without merely packaging the same errors more efficiently?

translations.label: exec meaning
translations.text: Keep most of the magic, lose most of the bill.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Training a smaller model to mimic a stronger one closely enough to be cheaper and still useful.

entries.note: Distillation is often described as model compression, but the interesting part is not just size. It is which behaviours survive the squeeze.
entries.body: The smaller model may be faster, cheaper, and easier to deploy. It is still carrying somebody else's worldview in a lighter suitcase.

entries.title: Elon Musk
entries.devilDefinition: What happens when AI acquires a promoter, a distribution engine, and a permanent ability to turn product discussion into spectacle.
entries.plainDefinition: Elon Musk is the founder of xAI and one of the most polarising public figures in AI. In practice, his significance in AI discourse comes from a mix of capital, platform reach, engineering ambition, political attention, and narrative force.
entries.whyExists: Because Musk is never merely a person in these discussions. His name functions as a compressed argument about speed, attention, ecosystem control, ideological posture, and whether theatrical force is itself a strategic asset.
entries.misuse: By treating him as either pure substance or pure noise. The useful position is less flattering to everyone's instincts: the spectacle matters because attention, distribution, and organisational velocity all materially affect how AI products land.
entries.practicalMeaning: When people invoke Elon Musk in AI, they usually mean xAI's ambitions, the Grok ecosystem, or the broader reality that infrastructure, media reach, and public controversy can be bundled into one strategic instrument.
entries.example: "Once Elon entered the product discussion, it acquired three new plotlines, a stronger ideological charge, and a lower chance of remaining about the product."
askNext: Is the relevance technical, political, commercial, or cultural?
Are they talking about xAI's systems, Musk's distribution power, or both?
Is this claim about model quality, market attention, or theatre with infrastructure attached?
entries.warningLabel: Attention is not the same thing as technical evidence, but it would also be foolish to pretend attention does not matter.

translations.label: media meaning
translations.text: A guarantee that the topic will become bigger, louder, and less tidy within minutes.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A shorthand for xAI plus the surrounding machinery of audience, controversy, capital, and platform leverage.

entries.note: Date-sensitive point: xAI announced on February 2, 2026 that SpaceX had acquired xAI. For that reason, it is safer to treat Musk here as xAI's founder and dominant public force than to casually repeat older standalone-company titles without context.
entries.body: With some founders, the product explains the persona. With Musk, the persona becomes part of the product whether anyone requested that feature or not.

entries.title: Embeddings
entries.devilDefinition: The act of turning words, sentences, or images into coordinates so that "similar enough" can be calculated instead of argued about.
entries.plainDefinition: Embeddings are numerical vector representations of data, such as text or images, designed so that semantically similar items are located near one another in vector space. They are commonly used for retrieval, clustering, recommendation, and deduplication.
entries.whyExists: Exact keyword matching is often too brittle for messy human language. Embeddings exist so systems can compare meaning approximately rather than matching only literal words.
entries.misuse: They are sometimes spoken about as if they were compact containers of all meaning, truth, and business strategy. They are not. An embedding is useful geometry, not metaphysics. Its quality depends on the model, the data, the task, and the comparison method.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means encoding text into vectors, storing those vectors somewhere searchable, and using nearest-neighbour techniques to find candidates that look semantically related. They are the quiet workhorses behind a lot of so-called magical retrieval.
entries.example: "The search improved after they switched from keywords to embeddings, not because the system got wiser, but because 'refund status' and 'where is my money' finally stopped looking unrelated."
askNext: What embedding model is being used, and for which task?
How are similarity, ranking, and filtering being evaluated?
What happens when semantically similar items are still operationally irrelevant?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A way of mapping data into numbers so retrieval can work by distance, not exact wording.

entries.body: Embeddings are one of the best examples of AI becoming less mysterious when you describe them properly. They are not memory crystals. They are useful numerical representations with clear limits.

entries.title: Eval Harness
entries.devilDefinition: The machinery teams build once they realise strong opinions about model quality are not, in fact, a release process.
entries.plainDefinition: An eval harness is the framework or runner used to execute repeatable evaluations against models, prompts, tools, or agent workflows and collect comparable results.
entries.whyExists: It exists because manual spot checks do not scale, model behaviour drifts, prompts regress quietly, and agent systems fail in more ways than a demo can comfortably admit.
entries.misuse: The harness gets mistaken for truth when it only reflects the cases you bothered to encode. A tidy dashboard is still compatible with spectacular blind spots.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means versioned datasets, scorers, task traces, cost accounting, regression thresholds, and a growing pile of awkward examples nobody wants to drop because they keep catching something embarrassing.
entries.example: "The eval harness did not make the agent good. It merely prevented the team from declaring victory after each plausible-looking run."
askNext: Which real failures are represented in the suite, and which remain folklore?
What metrics are being scored automatically, and what still needs human judgement?
Does the harness catch regressions before release, or just document them after the fact?

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The repeatable test rig standing between a model change and an avoidable incident.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A structured way to replay known cases often enough that quality becomes an argument with evidence.

entries.body: Every mature stack eventually invents a harness. AI teams simply get there after calling the same failure "surprising" three times.

entries.title: Eval-Driven Development
entries.devilDefinition: The radical idea that if your product depends on stochastic behaviour, you should probably measure something before calling the demo a platform.
entries.plainDefinition: Eval-driven development is the practice of using targeted evaluations to guide prompt changes, model selection, tool design, and release decisions for AI systems.
entries.whyExists: Traditional software tests do not fully capture AI behaviour, while pure vibes scale poorly once money, customers, or reputations are involved. Teams need a repeatable way to detect regressions and compare alternatives.
entries.misuse: It is misused when a handful of flattering examples are promoted to an evaluation strategy, or when teams treat proxy metrics as if they were the product itself. An eval can discipline judgement. It cannot replace it.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curated test sets, grading rubrics, automated checks, and before-after comparisons tied to real tasks. The mature version influences release gates. The immature version produces a dashboard nobody trusts but everyone screenshots.
entries.example: "Eval-driven development arrived the day they stopped arguing about prompts in Slack and started arguing about failure rates instead."
askNext: Which behaviours matter enough to measure continuously?
Do the evals reflect production tasks, or merely the tasks that are easiest to score?
What happens when the metric improves but the user experience quietly worsens?
entries.warningLabel: A useful discipline, especially when it resists becoming a PowerPoint ritual.

translations.label: team meaning
translations.text: Define the failure cases and score them before changing the system.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Replacing prompt debates with measurable arguments, then discovering the measurements also need supervision.

entries.body: The concept is sound. The danger begins when the organisation assumes that once a number exists, the thinking has been done.

entries.title: Evals
entries.devilDefinition: The discipline of finding out whether the system works before reality does it for you in public.
entries.plainDefinition: Evals are structured evaluations used to measure model or system performance against defined criteria. They may include benchmark tasks, human review, automated checks, red-team cases, regression tests, or task-specific business metrics.
entries.whyExists: Models are stochastic, context-dependent, and often impressive right up until they are deployed. Evals exist to replace anecdote with repeatable evidence and to catch regressions when prompts, models, or orchestration change.
entries.misuse: The word gets thrown around when teams have a benchmark slide and a lot of faith. Real evals are continuous, tied to the actual task, and uncomfortable enough to surface failure. If the evaluation only confirms what the team already hoped, it may be marketing in a lab coat.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curated test sets, scored outputs, human raters, regression suites, production monitoring, and painful arguments about what "good enough" means for this use case. Good evals are part of product operations, not just research hygiene.
entries.example: "They thought the release was safe because the demo looked smoother. The evals later showed the new prompt quietly doubled the rate of invented citations."
askNext: What failure modes does this eval suite actually test?
How often is it run, and what changes trigger regression checks?
Are we measuring benchmark elegance or real task performance?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A disciplined way of checking whether the system still deserves confidence after you changed something.

entries.body: The most revealing evals are usually the least glamorous ones: domain cases, nasty edge conditions, and boring regression checks that stop the product from drifting into fiction.

entries.title: Fine-tuning
entries.devilDefinition: Taking a large model and sending it to finishing school so it stops answering like the open internet and starts answering like your use case.
entries.plainDefinition: Fine-tuning is the process of further training a pre-existing model on a narrower dataset so that its behaviour shifts toward a specific domain, style, format, or task.
entries.whyExists: General-purpose models are broad, not bespoke. Fine-tuning exists for cases where prompting alone is too unstable, too expensive, or too weak to reliably shape behaviour.
entries.misuse: It is often sold as the universal answer whenever prompts disappoint. In reality, many problems blamed on a lack of fine-tuning are retrieval problems, product design problems, or evaluation problems. Fine-tuning can help, but it can also overfit and create fresh failure modes.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means curating a task-specific dataset, deciding how much of the base model to adapt, training on that smaller dataset, and checking whether the resulting behaviour improved enough to justify the effort and operational complexity.
entries.example: "They did not fine-tune because the team loved custom models; they did it because the required output format had to be boringly consistent."
askNext: What behaviour are we trying to change that prompting cannot hold steady?
What data will teach that behaviour without teaching unwanted shortcuts?
How will we know the tuned model is better, not just narrower?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A targeted attempt to move model behaviour with additional training rather than endless prompt cajoling.

entries.body: Good fine-tuning is usually boring in the best way. It removes repeated prompt hacks and makes behaviour steadier. Bad fine-tuning makes a model narrower, more brittle, and harder to reason about.

entries.title: Frontier Model
entries.devilDefinition: A model said to be at the frontier, which often means "very capable, very expensive, and deeply useful to mention in a fundraising narrative."
entries.plainDefinition: A frontier model is generally understood to mean one of the most capable and resource-intensive models available at a given time, usually near the top end of performance on a range of tasks. It is not a sharply defined scientific category.
entries.whyExists: The term exists because the field needed a way to talk about the leading edge of capability without listing every model individually. It can be useful shorthand when discussing high-end systems, their risks, and their costs.
entries.misuse: It is often treated as a formal badge rather than a loose ranking label. Vendors, commentators, and investors may use it to imply inevitability, superiority, or strategic importance beyond what the actual use case warrants.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a large, expensive model family with broad capability, strong benchmarks, and significant operational or policy attention. For many products, the question is not whether a model is frontier. The question is whether it is necessary.
entries.example: "The company pitched a frontier-model strategy. Operations translated that into a calmer sentence about high cost, external dependency, and unclear product fit."
askNext: Frontier by which metric: benchmark spread, multimodal breadth, tool use, or cost-to-quality ratio?
Does the use case actually need a top-tier model, or just a reliable one?
What trade-offs come with using the most capable model available?
entries.warningLabel: Useful shorthand, not a formal category. Often as much status signal as technical description.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: This company is close enough to the top of the capability race to sound strategically important.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A high-end model near the current performance frontier, whether or not your workload needs that much firepower.

entries.body: The term becomes most useful when followed immediately by the phrase "for what task?" Otherwise it tends to drift into status perfume.

entries.title: Function Calling
entries.devilDefinition: Giving the model permission to request an action, while reminding everyone that requesting an action is not the same as doing it safely.
entries.plainDefinition: Function calling is a pattern where a model is asked to produce structured arguments for a predefined tool or function, and the surrounding application then executes that tool in ordinary software.
entries.whyExists: Models are good at interpreting language but bad at directly mutating real systems without control. Function calling exists to separate decision-making about which action to take from the actual execution of that action.
entries.misuse: It gets described as if the model itself is performing the external action. Usually it is not. The model proposes the call. Your software validates it, executes it, and handles the consequences. If that surrounding code is sloppy, the elegance of the schema will not save you.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means JSON schemas, argument validation, permission controls, retries, and post-tool reasoning. Useful systems treat function calling as structured intent, not an excuse to trust a model near production infrastructure.
entries.example: "The assistant did not refund the customer by itself; it produced a structured request, the service checked policy, and only then did the refund tool run."
askNext: Who validates the arguments before execution?
What tools is the model allowed to call, and under what conditions?
How are failures, partial successes, and risky actions surfaced?

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model emits a structured request; the real system still has to decide whether to trust, reject, or execute it.

entries.body: Function calling is one of the clearest places where AI product language can mislead. The impressive part is not merely that the model chose a tool. The important part is whether the surrounding system kept that choice legible and safe.

entries.title: Gemini
entries.devilDefinition: A name used for models, apps, and ecosystem positioning, which keeps everyone slightly unsure whether they are comparing software, model families, or brand architecture.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini is Google's brand used across both AI models and user-facing products. Depending on context, it may refer to a model family, an assistant experience, or a broader product layer within Google's ecosystem.
entries.whyExists: The term matters because Google uses Gemini across several surfaces. Without context, claims about Gemini can blur model behaviour, app experience, workspace integration, and vendor strategy.
entries.misuse: It gets treated as if a result seen in one Google surface automatically describes every Gemini model or deployment context. That is rarely true. Tooling, integration, context, and product constraints all change the experience.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means either a Google model family or a Google product experience using those models. Serious comparison requires pinning down which one.
entries.example: "The team said Gemini handled the task well. After some digging, this meant a specific workspace flow, not a portable judgement about every Gemini model."
askNext: Do you mean the Gemini app, a Gemini model family, or a Google product using Gemini behind the scenes?
Which version or surface produced the result?
What tool access or integration context shaped the outcome?
entries.warningLabel: The brand spans models and products. Ask which one is being discussed.
entries.note: Gemini conversations often confuse integrated product behaviour with base model ability. Those are related, but not identical, claims.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A Google-branded AI surface whose exact meaning depends on which product or model someone is referring to.

entries.body: The safest follow-up sentence is short: "Which Gemini?" It rescues a surprising number of meetings.

entries.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live
entries.devilDefinition: The voice model meant to make real-time conversation feel less like alternating voicemail and more like software that noticed you were still speaking.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini 3.1 Flash Live is Google's real-time audio and voice model for live dialogue, voice-first agents, and conversational experiences that need lower latency and stronger turn-taking.
entries.whyExists: Spoken AI feels broken the moment latency, interruptions, or tonal comprehension fall behind human patience. A live audio model exists because real-time interaction is less forgiving than text and far less polite about failure.
entries.misuse: It gets marketed as natural conversation when many deployments are still glorified phone trees with better diction. Smoother audio helps. It does not automatically produce better reasoning, better policies, or a better answer.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means voice agents, customer-support flows, Search Live, and assistant products where handling interruption, noisy environments, and conversational rhythm matters as much as the content of the reply.
entries.example: "Flash Live made the assistant sound far more human, which only sharpened the need for the underlying judgement to improve as well."
askNext: Does it handle interruptions and background noise reliably?
Which gains come from the model itself, and which come from surrounding orchestration?
Are we building a useful voice agent, or simply a smoother queue?
entries.warningLabel: A pleasant voice is not the same thing as good judgement.

translations.label: developer meaning
translations.text: A faster, more robust live audio model for building voice-first experiences.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The same assistant ambition, but with less lag between misunderstanding and sound.

entries.body: Realtime AI is a harsher examiner than chat. The pauses are shorter, the mistakes are louder, and the illusion of competence has less time to hide.

entries.title: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite
entries.devilDefinition: The cheaper model tier whose chief virtue is letting ambitious product managers scale their optimism by the million tokens.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is Google's lower-cost, lower-latency Gemini 3.1 model for high-volume workloads where throughput and unit economics matter more than maximal frontier capability.
entries.whyExists: Most production AI systems are constrained less by grand theory than by latency budgets, API spend, and how much traffic the business intends to survive. A cheaper fast model tier exists because the invoice also attends the architecture review.
entries.misuse: It gets framed as though cheaper automatically means strategically sufficient. Sometimes it does. Sometimes it simply spreads weaker judgement across more requests and gives procurement a pleasant afternoon.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means translation, moderation, UI generation, bulk inference, and the usual industrial workloads where modest capability improvements are less important than response speed and cost discipline.
entries.example: "Flash-Lite was exactly the right model until someone quietly expanded the use case from 'classify this' to 'think for us'."
askNext: Is the bottleneck latency, token cost, or answer quality?
Are we choosing the smallest model that clears the bar, or the cheapest one that lets the slide deck survive?
What errors become acceptable once the per-request price drops?

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A cost-efficient model tier meant for workloads where volume matters more than dramatic cleverness.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A sensible default for many production calls, right up to the moment someone asks it to be profound.

entries.body: Model progress now arrives in at least two currencies: capability and affordability. Flash-Lite is the reminder that the second one decides far more deployments than the keynote usually admits.

entries.title: Gemma
entries.devilDefinition: Google's way of demonstrating that it, too, can participate in the open-model conversation without misplacing the rest of the product empire.
entries.plainDefinition: Gemma is Google's family of lighter-weight models released for developers to run, fine-tune, or adapt outside the company's main hosted product surfaces.
entries.whyExists: Many teams want smaller models for local use, private deployment, research, or fine-tuning. Google also benefits from having a developer-facing model family that can circulate beyond closed API products and keep it visible in the open-model ecosystem.
entries.misuse: It gets confused with Gemini, treated as a single model, or described as if "small" automatically means cheap, safe, or easy to deploy. As usual, the useful details live in the specific variant, size, licence terms, and workload.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice Gemma usually means a family of relatively lightweight Google models for experimentation, local inference, edge deployment, and fine-tuning work where a giant hosted model would be excessive, costly, or simply inconvenient.
entries.example: "They chose Gemma for the local prototype because the requirement was responsiveness on-device, not a spiritual commitment to the largest model available."
askNext: Which Gemma variant and size are we evaluating?
Does the workload actually benefit from a smaller local model, or are we just allergic to APIs?
How do quality, latency, and privacy trade-offs compare with hosted alternatives on the real task?
entries.warningLabel: Model family, not a single stable answer to the question.

translations.label: builder meaning
translations.text: A practical Google-backed model family for people who need something smaller and more portable than the flagship spectacle.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A lightweight model family that still needs task-level evaluation instead of brand-level trust.

entries.note: Gemma matters partly because it sits in the space between frontier-model marketing and the unromantic realities of shipping smaller systems.
entries.body: The glamour of large models tends to dominate the conversation. The invoices, device limits, and latency targets keep sending people back to families like this.

entries.title: GEO
entries.devilDefinition: SEO after a rebrand, a haircut, and a fresh promise to investors.
entries.plainDefinition: An emerging term for trying to increase a brand's visibility inside AI-generated answers, summaries, and recommendation layers rather than only in traditional search engine rankings.
entries.whyExists: Because people noticed that users increasingly ask chat systems instead of typing ten blue links into a search box.
entries.misuse: By pretending it is a wholly new discipline rather than a messy blend of discoverability, authority, structured content, citations, technical hygiene, and brand presence.
entries.practicalMeaning: Creating clear, well-structured, cited, machine-legible content that AI systems and retrieval layers can understand, quote, or prefer.
entries.example: "The agency sold GEO as a revolution, then quietly delivered schema, better copy, stronger pages, and clearer source material."
askNext: Which engines or assistants do they mean?
Are they measuring citations, referral traffic, mentions, or conversions?
Is this genuinely new work, or renamed SEO plus PR?
entries.warningLabel: Often a rebranding exercise with some real technical hygiene hiding underneath.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Make your content clearer, more structured, more citeable, and easier for retrieval layers to find without pretending the entire discipline was born yesterday.

entries.body: The suspicion is usually justified. Much of GEO is indeed old work wearing new shoes. That does not make it fake. It makes it familiar.

entries.title: Google Antigravity
entries.devilDefinition: Google's answer to the question of whether vibe coding needed a launch name with more lift.
entries.plainDefinition: Google Antigravity is Google's agentic development surface for building and editing apps from prompts, with deeper project understanding and integrations for backend, secrets, and deployment-adjacent work.
entries.whyExists: Prompt-to-app demos stop being charming once users want authentication, databases, API keys, multiplayer state, and software that can survive contact with actual requirements. Antigravity exists to move that promise closer to full-stack work.
entries.misuse: It is pitched as though software difficulty now consists mainly of choosing a framework and describing the vibes with conviction. The agent can scaffold more of the stack. It cannot repeal complexity, ownership, or future maintenance.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it means Google AI Studio getting more serious about app generation: Firebase wiring, tool use, framework selection, project memory, and a product surface where the agent is meant to feel like a teammate rather than a chat box with keyboard privileges.
entries.example: "Antigravity got the prototype airborne quickly, then left the team to rediscover that landing is its own discipline."
askNext: Is it generating maintainable software or just compressing the prototype stage?
How much of the workflow is genuinely agentic rather than pre-scripted scaffolding?
What happens once the app needs architecture rather than acceleration?

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: A Google surface for turning prompts into real apps with more backend seriousness than a weekend demo.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A stronger prompt-to-app workflow that still leaves the long-term engineering bill intact.

entries.body: Every platform eventually tries to become the place where software begins. Antigravity is Google's way of insisting that the beginning should already look like an agent.

entries.title: Google DeepMind
entries.devilDefinition: The research empire formed when Google looked at one of the most storied AI labs in the world and decided it would also like the rest of the machine under the same roof.
entries.plainDefinition: Google DeepMind is Google's central AI research and frontier-model organisation. It combines the original DeepMind organisation with key Google AI research efforts, and sits behind research programmes and products including Gemini, AlphaFold, robotics work, safety research, and broader frontier-model development.
entries.whyExists: Because people still say "DeepMind" as if it were a free-floating research monastery. It is now a major unit inside Google, with both scientific prestige and the platform power that comes from sitting inside one of the largest technology companies on earth.
entries.misuse: By treating Google DeepMind as either a pure research institution or merely another product division. It is both more interesting and more awkward than that: a frontier lab nested inside a company that wants research breakthroughs to become products at scale.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means Google's long-horizon AI engine: the part of the company responsible for major research advances, frontier models, safety work, and many of the technical foundations that later surface through Gemini and other Google products.
entries.example: "The pitch started with Gemini, but the strategic argument was really about Google DeepMind and whether one company could still dominate both research legitimacy and product distribution."
askNext: Do they mean Google DeepMind the lab, Gemini the model family, or the Gemini app?
Is this a research claim, a product claim, or a capability claim?
Are we discussing scientific output, safety posture, or Google's platform strategy?
entries.warningLabel: Research lab, product engine, and corporate strategy node. Ask which layer the speaker means.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: The place people point when they want the conversation to sound more scientific than commercial.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Google's frontier AI organisation, where research prestige and product ambition share the same building.

entries.note: Google's own framing emphasises responsible AI for broad human benefit, but the practical significance of Google DeepMind is that it is both a frontier research lab and a core engine of product advantage across Google's ecosystem.
entries.body: The old mental model of DeepMind as a separate mythic lab is now mostly nostalgia. The current reality is institutional power with far shorter distance to product.

entries.title: GPU
entries.devilDefinition: The rectangle of silicon that turns abstract AI ambition into rack diagrams, queueing pain, and a line item large enough to sober up strategy decks.
entries.plainDefinition: A GPU is a highly parallel processor originally built for graphics work that is now widely used to train and serve AI models because it handles the underlying matrix computations efficiently.
entries.whyExists: Because modern AI workloads need a great deal of parallel numerical work, and GPUs became the practical engine for that before most organisations had learned how physical their software plans were.
entries.misuse: By using "GPU" to mean the whole AI stack, or by assuming that access to GPUs automatically solves latency, data quality, product design, or cost discipline. Hardware helps. It does not finish the argument.
entries.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means a constraint on training speed, inference cost, memory limits, model size, procurement timing, and whether your supposedly simple launch now depends on specialist infrastructure.
entries.example: "The roadmap called it an AI feature. Finance called it six more GPUs and several uncomfortable follow-up questions."
askNext: Is the bottleneck raw compute, memory, networking, or utilisation?
Do we need premium GPU capacity, or are we overbuying for the workload?
What part of the user value actually depends on this level of hardware?

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The compute unit that decides whether your AI ambitions are shipping, waiting, or becoming procurement folklore.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A parallel processor that underwrites much of modern AI training and inference, along with a large share of the bill.

entries.note: The important distinction is not only which GPU you have, but whether the surrounding system can keep it fed, cooled, scheduled, and worth the money.
entries.body: A GPU is often described as the engine of AI. More usefully, it is the point where the conversation stops being metaphor and starts being capacity planning.
